title: Should the Fed tighten monetary policy in 2026?

Happy Tuesday out there team 42. It's
your skipper here Darius Dale presenting
our macro minute for Tuesday, July 14th,
2026. As always, we'll start with the
executive summary from today's lead up
morning note. So, let's dive right in.
Today's key macro question is, should
the Fed tighten monetary policy in 2026?
The short answer is, should they? No.
Will they? Perhaps. As our new
multi-factor Fed decision tree model
signaled last week, the Fed should
remain on hold over the next year.
Still, the FOMC may opt to play action
pass, i.e. tighten cyclically to set up
the run, i.e. ease structurally, because
Chair Warsh likely has substantial
easing planned for 2027 and 2028.
Regaining some credibility on its price
stability mandate now will create scope
for much more Fed easing than is
currently priced in or can be credibly
priced in over the medium term without
it. In terms of a little nuance, the
June CPI data supported the consensus
outlook for slowing inflation. The June
headline CPI and energy CPI data did not
support our sticky inflation theme. The
June food CPI data did. The June core
CPI, core goods CPI, core services CPI,
and super core CPI data did not support
our sticky inflation theme. The two
basis point contribution to
month-over-month headline CPI from core
services is the lowest since September
2020 and well below its pre-COVID trend
of 14 basis points. This data materially
reduces the probability that the Warsh
Fed will {quote} play action pass to set
up the run. The 191 basis point
contribution to year-over-year headline
CPI from core services represents a
three-month low. Although it is
structurally elevated relative to its
pre-COVID trend of 170 basis points, it
is moving in the right direction and
well below levels that we believe would
signal prospective tightening if it were
accelerating, i.e. greater than or equal
to 250 basis points. Should the Fed
tighten over the medium term? On net,
our analysis of the interconnected
amorphous drivers of inflation currently
signals the Fed should remain on hold.
If the interconnected amorphous drivers
of inflation currently signal the Fed
should remain on the
then why has the FOMC become so
rhetorically hawkish? We surmise it's to
regain some credibility on its price
stability mandate before proceeding with
substantial easing and financial
repression throughout 2027 and 2028. For
what it's worth, US dollar money markets
currently view the Fed's policy rate
setting as modestly accommodative
according to the 42 macro market implied
Fed neutral rate model. It must hike by
one to two times to get back to neutral.
US dollar money markets are currently
pricing in slightly more than one rate
hike over the next 12 months.
As always, we'll wrap up with a question
from our community. This one's titled is
AI CapEx a bubble? It says a growing but
still small group of investors and
analysts are pushing back against the
prevailing doom scenario around CapEx
spending. While there are several
arguments used to justify this
contrarian point of view, the one common
and persuasive point is that the
hyperscalers have crossed the
probability threshold or profitability
threshold rather, where profits are
exceeding CapEx. The other main point
seems to be in the definition of a
bubble. Traditionally, a bubble is
formed by skewed markets where some
non-market player or force has its thumb
on the scale, which incurs a
misallocation of funds. The argument
made is that this is not true of AI
CapEx and that it is being driven by
market forces alone. The details seem
quite persuasive in showing this to be
the reality. So, what are your thoughts?
So, my thoughts is we're very clearly in
AI CapEx bubble. This is one of the
largest demand shocks in in in in
recorded economic history, not just in
the US economy but in in the in the
global economy. Just putting some
numbers on that,
the current projection for $800 of CapEx
in 2026 is about 2 and 1/2% of GDP. The
it's projected to accelerate a foreign
billion dollars from there to about, you
know, 3 and 1/2% of GDP next year. I
mean, these are ridiculous numbers. I
mean, so like, you know, the the
starting point is you know, whenever you
get into the ridiculous number zone, you
have to assume that, you know, things
are starting to become a bubble-like.
And
another thing I would say on the AI
CapEx bubble is that historically, you
know, kind of on global Wall Street, a
rule of thumb is that something doubles
in two or three years, you know,
investors tend to believe it's a bubble.
Um, I believe Sandra O'Connor Miller
quoted that uh back in the uh '80s or
'90s.
And so if something doubles in uh two to
three years, it's a bubble. Well, we've
seen AI CAPEX double year after year
after year for I think four years now.
Uh so,
uh that might also indicate it may be uh
a bubble. And then lastly, I I we
respectfully disagree with this view
that, you know, there's no non-market
player force that has its thumb on the
scale with with respect to misallocation
of funds. Have you guys seen the
president's AI directive from last
spring and all the moves that they've
made to essentially nationalize and
support the development of AI to win the
fabricated US versus China AI race.
Again, this is I mean, these companies
uh basically got a
uh wink wink nod nod uh
and a promise for favorable regulation
from the federal government to
accelerate and move as fast as they
possibly can uh to uh to to to to to put
all this CAPEX into the ground. Uh so,
you know, it's it's our belief that
there is a
a non-market force encouraging a
misallocation of funds here. And oh, by
the way, just think about Take a step
back.
Of course, we're in AI CAPEX bubble. And
you want to know why? It's because AI is
a transformative technology it it
on a scale rarely seen in human history.
We're talking about canals and railroads
and steam
internal combustion engines and and and
and consumer durable goods like autos
and radios and and and electricity and
and internet. Like, when something is
that good of an idea, everyone throws as
much money as they possibly can at the
good idea in order for hopes of
extracting forward ex ante market
returns from that good idea. And so,
that's the natural reaction is anytime
something is so obvious of a good idea,
it's going to attract an excess of
capital because it's such an obvious
good idea relative to every other thing
that we could possibly be invested in
right now. I mean, I guess you could go
invest in an old steel mill or or a
manufacturing company, but wouldn't you
rather invest in OpenAI and Anthropic
and and all the companies like Nvidia
and Micron and SK Hynix and Samsung that
support the development of of all of the
AI technology and all this CapEx. I
mean, think about it. It's always
relative to the menu of other investing
options. And right now, AI is such a
good idea relative to the other menu of
investing options that we have trillions
of dollars of CapEx being thrown at it
by investors who think they're going to
get a very high ex-ante return on a
timeline that justifies the
concentration risk in their portfolios.
Where where we disagree with that view
is that it's probably not going to
happen on the timeline to justify the
concentration risk on their portfolios
in the context of how much capital is
being thrown at it at the current
juncture. Not everybody's going to make
the amount of money they expect to make
over certain intervals in the coming
years, but that that's neither here nor
there for the context of this question.
So, we'll wrap it up there.
Darius Dale here representing our macro
man for Tuesday, July 14th, 2026.
Let's go France.
Cheers.
